heroOverview: Keratitis is inflammation of the cornea — the clear front window of the eye — and is one of the leading preventable causes of corneal blindness worldwide, responsible for an estimated 1.5–2 million new cases of monocular blindness annually. The condition encompasses infectious forms (bacterial, viral, fungal, amoebic) and non-infectious forms (autoimmune, exposure, neurotrophic), all of which damage the densely innervated corneal epithelium and stroma. ICD-10 code H16.9 is assigned when keratitis is confirmed clinically but the specific aetiology has not yet been classified. Even a limited episode can leave stromal scarring that permanently reduces visual acuity; rapid specialist assessment within 24 hours of onset is the central determinant of whether vision is preserved. Contact lens wear is the dominant modifiable risk factor in high-income countries, associated with up to 30,000 cases of infectious keratitis in the United States per year.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1–1.5 million cases of infectious keratitis occur annually in the United States; globally, 1.5–2 million become monocular blind from corneal disease each year (WHO)
keyStats.demographics: Contact lens wearers account for the majority of infectious keratitis cases in high-income countries; agricultural workers and the immunocompromised are disproportionately affected in lower-income settings
keyStats.avgAge: Working-age adults (20–50) are most commonly affected in developed countries; no age is exempt
keyStats.globalCases: Corneal disease — of which keratitis is the leading acute cause — is the fourth leading cause of global blindness after cataract, glaucoma, and trachoma

definition: Keratitis, unspecified (ICD-10: H16.9) is inflammation of the corneal epithelium, Bowman's layer, stroma, Descemet's membrane, or endothelium without a currently specified aetiology. The cornea is avascular, deriving oxygen from the tear film and aqueous humour; this limits its immune response but also means that even modest microbial load or immune activation can rapidly damage tissue. Infectious keratitis is most commonly bacterial (Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus predominate in contact-lens users), herpetic (herpes simplex virus type 1 is the most frequent viral cause in temperate climates), fungal (Fusarium and Aspergillus in tropical settings and trauma with vegetable matter), or amoebic (Acanthamoeba in contact lens users exposed to non-sterile water). Non-infectious keratitis arises from autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (peripheral ulcerative keratitis), severe ocular surface disease (exposure keratopathy from incomplete lid closure), neurotrophic keratitis (fifth cranial nerve damage ablating the corneal protective reflex), and toxicity from topical medications. Corneal scarring from any form of keratitis can permanently reduce visual acuity and lead to irregular astigmatism or corneal opacification. Management is ophthalmology-led and urgent.

typesClassification.description: The most common form in contact-lens wearers in high-income countries; Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus are the leading pathogens. Presents as a painful, rapidly progressive corneal ulcer with purulent infiltrate and hypopyon in severe cases.

Herpes simplex virus type 1 causes dendritic epithelial ulcers recognisable by their branching morphology; recurrent episodes can progress to stromal keratitis, disciform keratitis, and irreversible scarring.

Fusarium and Candida species; more common after agricultural trauma or in immunocompromised patients. Characterised by a feathery infiltrate border and satellite lesions; responds slowly to antifungal therapy and carries a poor visual prognosis if delayed.

A protozoan infection almost exclusively in contact lens wearers who use non-sterile water; severe pain disproportionate to initial signs, ring infiltrate in late stages. Notoriously misdiagnosed as herpetic keratitis in early presentation.
Results from incomplete corneal coverage by the eyelids (lagophthalmos), exposing the inferior cornea to desiccation; causes punctate epithelial erosions and, if prolonged, stromal ulceration.

Loss of corneal sensation from trigeminal nerve damage (herpetic, traumatic, or post-surgical) removes the protective blink reflex and trophic support, causing indolent epithelial defects and stromal melt.

primarySymptoms: Severe, persistent eye pain or aching — often described as a feeling of grit or a foreign body that does not resolve with blinking or irrigation.
Marked photophobia — intense sensitivity to light that causes involuntary eyelid closure, often more severe than in conjunctivitis.
Excessive tearing and watery discharge, sometimes with mucoid or purulent material depending on the causative agent.
Pronounced redness of the eye, typically circumcorneal (ciliary flush) rather than diffuse, indicating deep ocular involvement.
Blurred or hazy vision due to epithelial oedema, infiltrate opacity, or tear film disruption overlying the cornea.
Visible white or grey spot or ulcer on the cornea, often apparent to the patient or an observer under direct light.
Eyelid swelling and difficulty opening the eye fully, caused by reflex blepharospasm in response to severe corneal pain.
Worsening symptoms with contact lens wear — contact lens-related keratitis is typically felt within hours of insertion.
Hypopyon — a visible level of white cells settled in the lower anterior chamber — in severe bacterial keratitis, indicating significant intraocular inflammation.
Reduced corneal sensation in neurotrophic or herpetic keratitis, which can paradoxically reduce pain while disease progresses.
earlyWarningSigns: A new foreign body sensation that does not clear with blinking or saline irrigation.
Mild photophobia or discomfort around bright lights developing over one or two days.
Contact lenses becoming increasingly uncomfortable to wear before frank pain develops.
A faint grey haze or small white spot visible on the corneal surface.
emergencySigns: Rapid increase in pain, redness, and vision loss over 12–24 hours — any keratitis can cause permanent blindness within days if untreated.
Visible pus in the lower anterior chamber (hypopyon), indicating severe bacterial or fungal infection with intraocular spread.
Complete loss of vision or light perception in the affected eye.
Corneal perforation — an irregularly shaped pupil or aqueous humour leaking from the eye indicates a surgical emergency.

causes.description: Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Staphylococcus aureus adhere to a contact-lens-disrupted or trauma-damaged corneal epithelium, secreting proteases and exotoxins that rapidly liquefy stromal collagen. Extended-wear contact lens use raises overnight exposure and bacterial load on the ocular surface dramatically.

HSV-1 establishes latency in the trigeminal ganglion after primary infection; stress, immunosuppression, UV exposure, or systemic illness triggers reactivation and anterograde transport to the cornea, where the virus replicates in epithelial cells, forming the pathognomonic dendritic ulcer.

Acanthamoeba cysts are ubiquitous in fresh water, soil, and tap water. Contact lens cases rinsed with tap water or worn in pools and showers allow trophozoite binding to lens-cleaned corneal epithelium, initiating an inflammatory reaction that progresses to ring infiltrate if not recognised early.

Penetrating injury from plant material, soil, or organic debris introduces Fusarium or Aspergillus spores directly into the cornea. Agricultural workers and gardeners are at highest risk; antifungal penetration into avascular corneal stroma is poor, making early diagnosis critical.

causes.cause: Ocular surface disease and dry eye
causes.description: Severe aqueous-deficient dry eye disrupts the tear film that provides antimicrobial proteins, oxygen, and nutrients to the epithelium; chronic desiccation causes punctate erosions that lower the infectious threshold substantially and predispose to sterile corneal melting.

Systemic corticosteroids, biologic immunosuppressants, HIV, and neutropenia impair the local immune response, making the cornea vulnerable to opportunistic organisms that would be cleared in healthy hosts. Rheumatoid arthritis can cause sterile peripheral ulcerative keratitis through deposition of immune complexes.

riskFactors.description: Extended-wear, overnight, or poorly-maintained contact lens use is the dominant risk factor for infectious keratitis in high-income countries; proper lens hygiene, avoiding water contact with lenses, and daily disposable use reduce risk by roughly 60–80%.

Vegetable matter, soil, and contaminated water introduced into the eye during agricultural work, gardening, or outdoor sports provide both the inoculum and the breach in the epithelial barrier required for infection.

HIV infection, haematological malignancy, prolonged systemic corticosteroids, and biologic therapies impair corneal immune defences, raising susceptibility to bacterial, fungal, and amoebic pathogens.

A history of herpes simplex keratitis predicts recurrence; approximately 10–20% of patients with a first episode have a recurrence within two years, rising with each episode. Oral aciclovir prophylaxis reduces recurrence risk by approximately 45%.

Inadequate tear film production reduces antimicrobial peptide concentration, disrupts corneal epithelial integrity, and removes the first physical barrier to microbial invasion; treatment of dry eye directly lowers keratitis risk.

Topical steroids suppress corneal immune responses and can reactivate latent HSV; inadvertent use on an undiagnosed herpetic keratitis — the most common prescribing error — can convert a dendritic ulcer into an amoeba-like stromal melt within days.

LASIK flap interfaces and post-keratoplasty grafts provide potential microbial entry points, alter corneal sensation, and may carry latent viral reservoirs; post-surgical patients require vigilant monitoring.

affectedDemographics: Contact lens wearers of all ages, particularly those wearing extended-wear or sleeping in lenses
Agricultural and outdoor workers in tropical and subtropical regions (fungal and amoebic keratitis)
Immunocompromised patients including those on long-term steroids, biologic therapy, or with HIV
People with a history of herpes simplex eye disease
Patients with severe dry eye, blepharitis, or incomplete lid closure
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with slit-lamp biomicroscopy — the cornerstone of corneal assessment — which characterises the location, depth, shape, and appearance of any epithelial defect, infiltrate, or ulcer, and evaluates for anterior chamber reaction and hypopyon. Fluorescein staining with cobalt blue illumination delineates epithelial defects: a branching (dendritic) pattern strongly suggests HSV keratitis, whereas a round, well-demarcated stromal infiltrate with surrounding haze points toward bacterial aetiology. Corneal sensation testing distinguishes neurotrophic from inflammatory causes. For ulcers larger than 2 mm, not clearly viral in morphology, or failing empirical antibiotic therapy, corneal scraping is essential: material is plated on blood agar, chocolate agar, Sabouraud agar (for fungi), and non-nutrient agar seeded with E. coli (for Acanthamoeba), and a smear is Gram-stained and examined for hyphae and cysts. Confocal microscopy provides non-invasive real-time imaging of the corneal layers and is particularly valuable for diagnosing Acanthamoeba (characteristic double-walled cysts) and fungal infections without scraping. PCR on corneal swabs or aqueous humour is increasingly standard for herpetic and Acanthamoeba keratitis when culture is negative. Anterior segment OCT characterises infiltrate depth and guides surgical planning in complicated cases. Systemic investigations — ESR, ANCA, rheumatoid factor, and complement levels — are warranted when peripheral ulcerative keratitis raises concern for systemic vasculitis or autoimmune disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the size, depth, and morphology of corneal epithelial defects and infiltrates, differentiating between bacterial, viral, fungal, and amoebic patterns and guiding management decisions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The ophthalmologist instils a fluorescein drop and examines your eye under a slit-lamp microscope with blue-filtered light; the procedure is painless and takes 10–15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Corneal scraping with culture and sensitivity
diagnosticTests.purpose: Isolates the causative organism and determines antibiotic or antifungal susceptibility, guiding targeted therapy when empirical treatment fails or when a fungal or amoebic cause is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under topical anaesthesia at a slit lamp, the clinician gently scrapes the edge of the ulcer with a sterile spatula; mild discomfort; results take 24–72 hours for bacteria and up to 4 weeks for fungi.

diagnosticTests.test: PCR on corneal swab or aqueous humour
diagnosticTests.purpose: Highly sensitive molecular detection of HSV, VZV, Acanthamoeba, and fungal DNA when cultures are negative or when the clinical picture is ambiguous.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile swab is passed across the ulcer surface or a small aqueous sample is taken by anterior chamber paracentesis; PCR results are available within 24–48 hours at specialist centres.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive, real-time imaging of corneal microstructure at cellular resolution; identifies Acanthamoeba cysts, fungal hyphae, and nerve fibre density changes without tissue sampling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe with a drop of coupling gel is gently placed close to the corneal surface; the examination takes 15–20 minutes and is well-tolerated.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects reduced corneal sensation caused by neurotrophic disease, herpetic neuropathy, or post-surgical denervation, which changes management toward neurotrophic keratitis protocols.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A wisp of dental floss or a Cochet-Bonnet aesthesiometer tip touches the central cornea; absence of a blink reflex indicates reduced sensation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides high-resolution cross-sectional imaging of the corneal layers to assess infiltrate depth, epithelial integrity, and stromal thinning without contact, aiding surgical planning and monitoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You rest your chin on a chin-rest while a non-contact laser scans the eye; the procedure is quick and painless, taking under five minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Systemic autoimmune screen (ESR, CRP, ANCA, RF)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies underlying systemic vasculitis or rheumatoid arthritis driving peripheral ulcerative keratitis, which requires systemic immunosuppression in addition to topical treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw from the arm; results take 24–48 hours and are reviewed by both ophthalmology and rheumatology in complex cases.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is urgency-stratified by aetiology, severity, and visual threat. For bacterial keratitis, intensive topical fluoroquinolone drops (ciprofloxacin or moxifloxacin every 30–60 minutes for the first 48 hours) are the standard of care, with culture results used to tailor therapy. Fortified antibiotic drops — cefazolin plus tobramycin — are added for severe or treatment-resistant cases. Herpetic epithelial keratitis responds to topical ganciclovir or trifluridine, with oral aciclovir for stromal disease. Fungal keratitis is treated with topical natamycin (filamentous fungi) or voriconazole (Candida and refractory cases) for at least four to six weeks, with systemic voriconazole for deep infections. Acanthamoeba keratitis requires prolonged combination therapy with polyhexamethylene biguanide (PHMB) and propamidine isethionate (Brolene) for months, making early diagnosis critical to outcome. Exposure and neurotrophic keratitis require addressing the underlying cause — tarsorrhaphy for lagophthalmos, preservative-free lubricants, and autologous serum drops for neurotrophic disease; recombinant human nerve growth factor (cenegermin) is approved for moderate to severe neurotrophic keratitis. Corticosteroid drops are used cautiously and only under close specialist supervision after infection is excluded or adequately controlled, to reduce stromal immune infiltration and scarring. Surgical options including corneal collagen cross-linking (for progressive bacterial ulcers), conjunctival flap, and penetrating or deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty are reserved for advanced or refractory disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical fluoroquinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, moxifloxacin)
medicalTreatments.description: Broad-spectrum bactericidal agents used as first-line for bacterial keratitis; instilled every 30–60 minutes initially, then tapered as the epithelial defect heals. Achieve high drug concentrations in corneal tissue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: First-line topical fluoroquinolones achieve clinical cure in approximately 85–90% of culture-positive bacterial keratitis when initiated promptly.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical ganciclovir or trifluridine
medicalTreatments.description: Nucleoside analogues that inhibit HSV DNA polymerase; applied five times daily for herpetic epithelial keratitis until epithelial healing, then tapered. Ganciclovir gel has fewer side effects and better patient adherence than trifluridine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HSV epithelial keratitis healing rates exceed 90% with antiviral therapy within 7–14 days; recurrence risk is reduced approximately 45% by maintenance oral aciclovir.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical natamycin 5%
medicalTreatments.description: A polyene antifungal with best activity against filamentous fungi (Fusarium, Aspergillus); the first-line topical agent for fungal keratitis based on the MUTT I trial evidence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the MUTT I trial (NEI-funded), natamycin achieved significantly better visual and infiltrate-size outcomes than voriconazole for Fusarium keratitis (OR 1.3 improvement).

medicalTreatments.name: PHMB and propamidine isethionate (for Acanthamoeba)
medicalTreatments.description: Combined antiseptic drops applied hourly during waking hours for at least three to six months; PHMB (0.02%) disrupts Acanthamoeba trophozoite membranes while propamidine provides cysticidal activity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined PHMB regimens achieve quiescence in approximately 85–90% of early-stage Acanthamoeba keratitis; late-stage cases with ring infiltrate have significantly worse outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Cenegermin (recombinant human nerve growth factor)
medicalTreatments.description: A topical eye drop approved for neurotrophic keratitis (stage 2 and 3) that promotes corneal epithelial healing by restoring trophic support; applied six times daily for eight weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the REPARO trial, cenegermin achieved complete corneal healing in 69.6% of patients at eight weeks versus 35% with vehicle, a clinically and statistically significant difference.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical corticosteroids (adjunctive)
medicalTreatments.description: Used under strict specialist supervision only after infection is controlled; reduce immune-mediated stromal infiltration and minimise scarring. Contraindicated in active fungal keratitis and used cautiously in herpetic stromal disease with concurrent antivirals.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive steroids reduce stromal scarring and hasten visual recovery in bacterial keratitis controlled by antibiotics, as demonstrated in the SCUT trial for ciprofloxacin-controlled Streptococcal keratitis.

surgicalOptions.name: Penetrating keratoplasty (corneal transplant)
surgicalOptions.description: Full-thickness replacement of the central cornea with donor tissue; indicated for corneal perforation, extensive scarring after infection, or failed medical treatment of severe keratitis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 85–90% graft survival at one year for keratitis-related transplants in low-risk eyes; significantly lower in vascularised or inflamed corneas.

surgicalOptions.name: Corneal collagen cross-linking
surgicalOptions.description: UV-A light combined with riboflavin eye drops stiffens corneal collagen, halting stromal melting in progressive bacterial or sterile ulcers and reducing perforation risk.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 70–80% of cases of progressive ulcers treated with cross-linking halt progression and avoid perforation in observational series.

surgicalOptions.name: Tarsorrhaphy
surgicalOptions.description: Partial or complete suturing of the eyelids to reduce corneal exposure in severe exposure or neurotrophic keratitis when medical treatment has failed to promote re-epithelialisation.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Highly effective for exposure keratitis when completed before stromal melt; re-epithelialisation rates above 90% in appropriate candidates.

alternativeTreatments.name: Autologous serum eye drops
alternativeTreatments.description: Derived from the patient's own blood serum, containing growth factors (EGF, TGF-beta), fibronectin, and vitamin A; used for neurotrophic keratitis and severe dry eye-related keratitis unresponsive to standard lubricants.

alternativeTreatments.name: Scleral contact lenses
alternativeTreatments.description: Large-diameter gas-permeable lenses that vault over the cornea and maintain a liquid reservoir, providing continuous corneal hydration and optical correction in irregular corneas after healing from keratitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Amniotic membrane transplantation
alternativeTreatments.description: Fresh or dried amniotic membrane placed on the corneal surface as a biological bandage reduces inflammation and promotes epithelial healing in persistent epithelial defects following keratitis.

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected keratitis warrants same-day ophthalmology assessment. Permanent vision loss from corneal scarring can occur within 24–48 hours of severe bacterial or Acanthamoeba infection. An ophthalmologist provides slit-lamp characterisation of the lesion, microbiological sampling, and initiation of targeted intensive topical therapy that cannot be replicated in primary care. Any delay in specialist involvement significantly worsens visual outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a cornea and external disease subspecialist — a corneal surgeon with training in infectious keratitis, confocal microscopy, and corneal surgery. For Acanthamoeba or fungal keratitis, centres with microbiology laboratories capable of non-nutrient agar culture and in vivo confocal microscopy achieve far better outcomes than general ophthalmology settings. For autoimmune keratitis, joint management with a rheumatologist is essential.
preventionStrategies: Handle contact lenses with scrupulous hygiene: wash hands before insertion, use only sterile lens solutions, never use tap water or saliva, and replace lenses and cases as directed.
Avoid sleeping in contact lenses; overnight lens wear increases bacterial keratitis risk 10- to 15-fold compared with daily removal.
Use protective eyewear when working with soil, vegetation, or in dusty or high-velocity debris environments.
Complete oral aciclovir prophylaxis if recommended after a first episode of HSV keratitis to prevent recurrence.
Treat dry eye and blepharitis early to restore the tear-film barrier against microbial invasion.
Never use topical corticosteroid eye drops without a confirmed diagnosis from an ophthalmologist; inadvertent steroid use on an infectious keratitis is a major cause of preventable vision loss.
lifestyleModifications: Switch to daily disposable contact lenses to eliminate lens-case contamination, the most common source of Acanthamoeba exposure.
Use well-fitting swimming goggles when swimming and remove contact lenses before water sports.
Take regular breaks from screen use and use preservative-free lubricating drops if you have dry eyes, as surface desiccation lowers the infectious threshold.
Avoid rubbing eyes after outdoor work or after handling soil and plants.
Attend all scheduled ophthalmology follow-up appointments during and after treatment; keratitis can progress silently after partial response to initial therapy.

recommended: Vitamin A-rich foods such as leafy greens, carrots, and dairy — vitamin A deficiency is a global cause of corneal xerosis and increased keratitis susceptibility
Omega-3 fatty acids (oily fish, flaxseed) to support tear film lipid layer quality and reduce dry eye-associated surface damage
Adequate zinc and vitamin C intake to support corneal tissue repair and collagen synthesis
avoid: Alcohol and tobacco, which impair immune function and wound healing and may delay corneal re-epithelialisation
High-sodium processed foods that may worsen systemic inflammation in autoimmune-related keratitis

exerciseGuidelines: No specific exercise restrictions apply to keratitis beyond protecting the eye from trauma and avoiding water contact during active infection. Swimming and contact sports should be avoided until the corneal epithelium has fully healed and the ophthalmologist has confirmed resolution. During treatment, avoid activities that cause heavy sweating near the eye, as salt in sweat can irritate and dilute intensive topical drops. Once healed, regular exercise is beneficial for immune function and general well-being.
dailyManagement: Apply intensive antibiotic, antiviral, or antifungal drops on the exact schedule prescribed — missing doses even briefly allows organisms to re-establish.
Keep the eye clean; wipe away any discharge with a clean, moist cloth but avoid vigorous rubbing.
Wear protective eyewear outdoors to shield the healing cornea from UV, dust, and trauma.
Do not apply any over-the-counter redness-relieving drops, which typically contain vasoconstrictors that can mask worsening signs.
Return to the ophthalmologist within 24–48 hours of starting treatment to confirm response, and attend every scheduled follow-up thereafter.
Do not reinsert contact lenses until explicitly cleared by the ophthalmologist — usually at least two weeks after complete epithelial healing.
prognosis: Visual outcome depends critically on the causative organism, the depth of corneal involvement at the time of first treatment, and the speed of specialist assessment. Superficial bacterial keratitis diagnosed and treated within 24 hours typically heals with minimal scarring and good visual recovery. Deeper ulcers, delayed treatment, or organisms such as Acanthamoeba, Pseudomonas, or fungi carry substantially worse prognoses: perforation occurs in approximately 1–10% of severe bacterial ulcers, and a large proportion of Acanthamoeba cases eventually require corneal transplantation. Viral herpetic keratitis has a recurrence rate of 27–50% within five years and cumulative scarring from repeated episodes is a major cause of corneal transplantation in high-income countries. Neurotrophic keratitis has a chronic course requiring lifelong management. Early diagnosis and pathogen-specific treatment remain the principal modifiable determinants of a good visual outcome.
recoveryTimeline: Bacterial keratitis typically shows measurable improvement — reduction in infiltrate size and re-epithelialisation — within three to five days of appropriate intensive topical therapy, with complete epithelial healing in two to three weeks for small lesions. Stromal scarring resolves partially over months; dense central scars may not clear. Herpetic epithelial keratitis heals within one to two weeks of antiviral therapy; stromal disease resolves over several weeks but may leave permanent haze. Fungal and Acanthamoeba keratitis require weeks to months of therapy and carry higher rates of permanent visual loss. After any episode, the corneal surface may remain irregular for three to six months, and spectacle or contact lens correction may need adjustment once the eye has stabilised.
complications: Corneal scarring and opacification — stromal infiltrate that leaves a permanent white scar reducing visual acuity; the most common long-term complication affecting an estimated 10–30% of moderate-to-severe cases.
Corneal perforation — occurs in fewer than 5–10% of severe or treatment-resistant ulcers but requires emergency surgical repair and risks permanent blindness.
Endophthalmitis — spread of infection to the vitreous cavity, a catastrophic complication with cure rates below 30% for visual recovery to functional levels.
Glaucoma — elevated intraocular pressure secondary to trabeculitis or steroid use during treatment; may require long-term medication or surgery.
Irregular astigmatism and monocular diplopia — residual corneal surface distortion after healing requiring specialist optical correction or therapeutic contact lenses.

supportResources.name: NHS — Keratitis
supportResources.description: UK guidance on keratitis symptoms, when to seek emergency eye care, and treatment overview.

supportResources.name: AAO EyeSmart — Infectious Keratitis
supportResources.description: American Academy of Ophthalmology patient education on recognising and preventing keratitis.

sources.title: Whitcher JP, Srinivasan M, Upadhyay MP. Corneal blindness: a global perspective. Bulletin of the World Health Organization, 2001
Prajna NV et al. The Mycotic Ulcer Treatment Trial (MUTT I): a randomized trial comparing natamycin vs voriconazole. JAMA Ophthalmology, 2013
Srinivasan M et al. Steroids for Corneal Ulcers Trial (SCUT): design and baseline characteristics. Archives of Ophthalmology, 2012

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection (systemic)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Systemic HSV infection involves mucocutaneous and visceral sites; herpetic keratitis is a specific ocular reactivation recognised by dendritic epithelial ulcers on fluorescein staining.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes severe eye pain, redness, and blurred vision like keratitis, but the cornea shows epithelial oedema without an epithelial defect and intraocular pressure is markedly elevated on measurement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Gonococcal keratitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gonococcal keratitis is a specific aetiology requiring dual antibiotic therapy and contact tracing; it is distinguished from unspecified keratitis by culture and STI testing rather than clinical appearance alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Herpetic keratitis (HSV)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Herpetic keratitis (B00.52) is an aetiology-specific code for confirmed HSV corneal disease; H16.9 is assigned before aetiology is confirmed or when aetiology remains unclassified.

relatedConditions.name: Herpes simplex infection
relatedConditions.relevance: HSV-1 is the most common cause of infectious keratitis in high-income countries and can cause recurrent episodes leading to progressive corneal scarring.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Keratitis-associated inflammation and steroid use during treatment raise intraocular pressure, and keratitis is a risk factor for secondary glaucoma.

relatedConditions.name: Herpetic keratitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most commonly specified subtype of keratitis; understanding its natural history, recurrence pattern, and prevention strategies is directly relevant to managing unspecified keratitis.

relatedConditions.name: Tuberculous keratitis
relatedConditions.relevance: An important differential in endemic regions and immunocompromised patients; interstitial keratitis with vessels in the mid-stroma may indicate TB or syphilitic aetiology requiring systemic workup.

relatedConditions.name: Zoster keratitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Varicella-zoster virus reactivation in the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve causes a distinctive keratitis with dendriform lesions and neurotrophic sequelae requiring antiviral therapy.

faqs.question: What is keratitis and how serious is it?
faqs.answer: Keratitis is inflammation of the cornea — the clear front surface of the eye. It ranges from mild superficial irritation to sight-threatening ulcers. Severe or untreated keratitis can cause permanent corneal scarring and blindness within days, so same-day ophthalmology assessment is essential.

faqs.question: What does keratitis feel like?
faqs.answer: Keratitis typically causes severe eye pain or a persistent gritty foreign-body sensation, intense light sensitivity, tearing, redness, and blurred vision. Pain is usually disproportionately severe compared with superficial eye conditions like conjunctivitis.

faqs.question: What causes keratitis?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are bacteria (particularly in contact lens wearers), herpes simplex virus, fungi (after eye injury with organic material), and Acanthamoeba (from non-sterile water contact with lenses). Non-infectious causes include dry eye, autoimmune disease, and incomplete eyelid closure.

faqs.question: Can contact lenses cause keratitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Contact lens wear — especially extended-wear, overnight use, or poor hygiene — is the leading cause of infectious keratitis in high-income countries, responsible for up to 30,000 cases per year in the US. Daily disposable lenses with strict hygiene reduce risk substantially.

faqs.question: How is keratitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause: intensive topical antibiotic drops (every 30–60 minutes initially) for bacterial keratitis; topical ganciclovir or oral aciclovir for herpetic keratitis; natamycin or voriconazole for fungal keratitis; and prolonged PHMB drops for Acanthamoeba. All require close ophthalmology monitoring.

faqs.question: How quickly does keratitis need to be treated?
faqs.answer: Bacterial keratitis can progress to corneal perforation within 48–72 hours. Same-day ophthalmology assessment is the standard of care for any suspected keratitis. Delay in starting targeted treatment is the primary driver of preventable corneal blindness from this condition.

faqs.question: Can keratitis cause permanent vision loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dense central corneal scarring from a severe or delayed keratitis episode can permanently reduce visual acuity and may require corneal transplantation to restore useful vision. Prompt, accurate treatment minimises this risk significantly.

faqs.question: How do I know if I have herpetic keratitis?
faqs.answer: Herpetic keratitis is diagnosed on slit-lamp examination by an ophthalmologist, who identifies a branching (dendritic) epithelial ulcer stained with fluorescein. A history of cold sores and recurrent painful red eye increases suspicion. PCR confirms the diagnosis when the picture is atypical.

faqs.question: Can keratitis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Herpetic keratitis recurs in approximately 27–50% of patients within five years. Bacterial and Acanthamoeba keratitis generally do not recur if the eye is healed and contact lens hygiene is corrected. Long-term oral aciclovir reduces HSV recurrence risk by approximately 45%.

faqs.question: Is keratitis contagious?
faqs.answer: Bacterial and viral (herpetic) keratitis are not directly transmitted via casual contact. HSV keratitis is caused by reactivation of the patient's own latent virus, not new acquisition. Acanthamoeba is acquired from the environment, not from other people.

faqs.question: Can I wear contact lenses after keratitis?
faqs.answer: Contact lens use must be suspended during active keratitis and resumed only on explicit clearance from the ophthalmologist, usually at least two weeks after complete epithelial healing. Daily disposable lenses with strict hygiene are preferred on return to lens wear.

faqs.question: What happens if keratitis is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated bacterial keratitis can progress to corneal perforation, endophthalmitis (infection inside the eye), and permanent blindness within days to weeks. Even mild untreated viral or amoebic keratitis can produce irreversible stromal scarring and corneal transplant-level vision loss.

faqs.question: How is Acanthamoeba keratitis different from other keratitis?
faqs.answer: Acanthamoeba keratitis causes disproportionately severe pain early, is almost exclusively linked to contact lens water exposure, is frequently misdiagnosed as herpetic keratitis, and requires months of combined antiseptic drops. Delayed diagnosis is the main cause of poor outcomes.

faqs.question: What is neurotrophic keratitis?
faqs.answer: Neurotrophic keratitis occurs when corneal sensation is lost — due to herpetic nerve damage, surgery, or facial nerve injury — removing the protective blink reflex and healing signals. The cornea develops chronic non-healing ulcers. Cenegermin eye drops can restore healing in moderate to severe cases.

faqs.question: Can dry eye cause keratitis?
faqs.answer: Severe dry eye disrupts the tear-film barrier and can cause punctate superficial keratitis, which in extreme cases progresses to sterile corneal ulceration. Treating dry eye reduces keratitis risk and speeds recovery from infectious episodes that occur on an already compromised surface.

faqs.question: What eye drops are used for bacterial keratitis?
faqs.answer: Ciprofloxacin 0.3% or moxifloxacin 0.5% drops are the standard first-line treatment for bacterial keratitis, applied every 30–60 minutes initially. For severe or resistant cases, fortified drops containing higher concentrations of cefazolin and tobramycin are compounded by specialist pharmacies.

faqs.question: Can swimming cause keratitis?
faqs.answer: Swimming while wearing contact lenses is the dominant risk factor for Acanthamoeba keratitis, because pool water, lakes, and rivers contain Acanthamoeba cysts. Wearing waterproof goggles and removing lenses before water contact significantly reduces this risk.

faqs.question: When should I go to emergency for keratitis?
faqs.answer: Attend an eye emergency unit on the same day for: severe eye pain, significant vision loss, a visible white spot on the cornea, or any keratitis symptoms in a contact lens wearer. Do not wait for a routine appointment — hours matter for preventing corneal scarring.

faqs.question: Can keratitis lead to a corneal transplant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 10–30% of moderate to severe keratitis cases develop enough corneal scarring to require penetrating keratoplasty (corneal transplant) for visual rehabilitation. Acanthamoeba and recurrent herpetic keratitis have the highest transplant rates.

faqs.question: How is keratitis different from conjunctivitis?
faqs.answer: Conjunctivitis (pink eye) is inflammation of the white membrane lining the eyelids and is usually mild, with discharge and diffuse redness but minimal pain and normal vision. Keratitis involves the cornea, causes severe pain and photophobia, reduces vision, and is potentially sight-threatening.

metaDescription: Keratitis is corneal inflammation causing severe eye pain, light sensitivity and vision loss. Learn causes, urgent treatment and prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
